g.data-narration: Welcome to Four-Variable Karnaugh Maps, a powerful method for simplifying digital logic circuits efficiently.
K-maps reduce gates, cost, and power consumption in digital circuits, making designs faster and more efficient.
A 4-variable K-map has 16 cells in a 4×4 grid, with Gray code labeling for proper cell adjacency.
The 4-variable K-map uses Gray code so adjacent cells differ by only one variable, enabling logical grouping.
g.text: Complete K-Map with Gray Code Labels
Gray code: each adjacent cell differs by exactly 1 bit

g.data-narration: Each cell represents a minterm from m₀ to m₁₅, calculated from the binary value of AB and CD combined.
The K-map wraps around both horizontally and vertically, so top connects to bottom and left to right.
Groups must be powers of 2, rectangular, as large as possible, and every 1-cell must be covered at least once.
A group of 2 eliminates 1 variable, group of 4 eliminates 2 variables, and group of 8 eliminates 3 variables.
g.text: Variables that CHANGE across the group are eliminated; constants remain in the product term

g.data-narration: Let us minimize F(A,B,C,D) = Σm(0, 2, 5, 7, 8, 10, 13, 15) using the K-map method step by step.
g.text: This means the function is 1 for minterms 0, 2, 5, 7, 8, 10, 13, and 15

g.data-narration: We plot the 1s at positions corresponding to the minterms in our function: 0,2,5,7,8,10,13,15.
g.text: Green cells = 1 (function is true), Red cells = 0 (don't care or false)

g.data-narration: We identify two groups of 4 cells each: one covers m5,m7,m13,m15 and another covers m0,m2,m8,m10.
Group 1 gives BD (B=1,D=1 constant) and Group 2 gives B'D' (B=0,D=0 constant), so F = BD + B'D'.
g.text: AB changes (01,11), CD changes (01,11) → B=1, D=1 constant
AB changes (00,10), CD changes (00,10) → B=0, D=0 constant
